purpose: Assesses where AI stands in the multi-stage process from task-level gains to aggregate TFP acceleration
components: Gate 1: Task-level productivity gains (cleared — demonstrable AI task acceleration)
Gate 2: Firm-level productivity gains (in progress — 15-25% of firms showing measurable gains)
Gate 3: Sectoral diffusion and competitive reallocation (not started)
Gate 4: Aggregate TFP acceleration visible in macro data (not started)
current_assessment: Gate 1 cleared, Gate 2 in progress at 15-25% of firms, Gates 3-4 not started. Aggregate TFP visibility requires all four gates to clear sequentially.
limitations: Cannot predict gate transition speeds
Assumes sequential progression when some parallelism may exist
Does not account for AI's potential to accelerate its own diffusion (recursive reorganization)
Historical base rates drawn from structurally different GPTs

purpose: Decomposes the AI-to-productivity lag into distinct phases with quantified historical durations
components: Phase 1: Diffusion — technology availability and initial adoption (shortest phase)
Phase 2: Reorganization — organizational restructuring to exploit technology (60-70% of total delay)
Phase 3: Measurement — statistical infrastructure captures the gains (varies by national statistics capacity)
current_assessment: AI is in late Phase 1 / early Phase 2. The reorganization phase is the binding constraint and has historically taken 60-70% of total lag duration across four GPT analogues.
limitations: Historical calibration from only 4 GPT analogues
Phase 2 duration may be compressible if AI assists its own organizational restructuring (recursive reorganization)
Measurement phase depends on statistical agency capacity and methodology, which varies by country
Phases overlap in practice — the sequential framing is a simplification

purpose: Identifies when hyperscaler capex spending becomes unsustainable, triggering retrenchment
components: Threshold 1: ROIC below WACC for 4+ consecutive quarters
Threshold 2: Capex/OCF ratio exceeding 55-65%
Kill zone requires BOTH thresholds to be crossed simultaneously
Derived from 5 historical analogues of capex cycles
current_assessment: Aggregate capex/OCF at 50-65% and approaching threshold. Meta and Amazon at or near 70%. At 30-50% capex growth rates, 2-3 hyperscalers could cross by late 2027. ROIC threshold harder to assess due to bundling.
limitations: 4 of 5 historical analogues were debt-funded — structurally different from self-funded hyperscalers
Self-funded entities may tolerate higher capex/OCF ratios
ROIC is nearly unmeasurable when AI is bundled into existing products
Prisoner's dilemma may prevent rational individual retrenchment before collective pain

purpose: Formalizes whether AI capex creates or destroys economic value added on a per-share basis
components: Legacy capital base (~$500B) at ~25% ROIC generating ~$77.5B EVA
AI invested capital (~$600B cumulative) at 3-12% ROIC
Bull case: AI capital generates $15B EVA (16% of total from 55% of capital)
Blended ROIC trajectory: ~25% declining to ~15-18%
current_assessment: Even in the bull case, AI capital is value-dilutive on a per-share basis. Revenue growth may mask the dilution in absolute terms but not in return-on-capital terms.
limitations: AI-specific ROIC is nearly unmeasurable when bundled into existing products
Revenue attribution between AI and legacy products is arbitrary
Specific magnitudes are illustrative, not precise (direction at 6/10, magnitudes at 4/10)
Does not capture option value of AI investment or strategic positioning benefits

purpose: Extends classical Minsky taxonomy to classify self-funded entities that systematically destroy per-share value
components: Hedge financing: income covers debt service and principal (classical)
Speculative financing: income covers interest but requires refinancing for principal (classical)
Ponzi financing: income insufficient for interest, dependent on asset appreciation (classical)
Hedge-destructive (new): solvent and self-funding but investing at sub-WACC returns, destroying per-share value over time
current_assessment: Hyperscaler AI capex may fit the hedge-destructive category: financially sound companies with strong OCF systematically investing in projects returning below cost of capital. Implies duration risk (slow grind) not drawdown risk (sudden stop).
limitations: Novel category — needs broader corroboration
Timing prediction limited — Japanese corporates sustained hedge-destructive behavior for decades
Requires ROIC measurement which is contested
Market may not reprice until the value destruction becomes undeniable in reported metrics

purpose: Links AI capex decisions to aggregate corporate profits and macro growth through accounting identity
components: Kalecki equation: Profits = Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports - Household Saving
Federal deficit (~$1.8-2T) as dominant profit sustainer
AI capex (~$250B) as marginal growth contributor
25% capex cut → ~$60-75B profit reduction → ~2.5-3% S&P 500 earnings impact
Four-channel demand decomposition: capital income, complementary labor, consumer surplus, fiscal redistribution
current_assessment: The Kalecki identity transforms AI capex from a sector story to a macro story. The fiscal deficit is doing the heavy lifting, but AI capex is the marginal contributor responsible for profit growth.
limitations: Accounting identity does not specify causation or dynamics
Assumes investment term is independent of other terms (may not hold if capex cuts trigger fiscal response)
Single-channel underconsumption arguments are insufficient — the four-channel decomposition is required
Does not predict timing of capex cuts

purpose: Predicts labor market outcomes based on the ratio of AI-augmented to AI-substituted tasks
components: Ratio >1.5:1 → net employment gains within 10 years
Ratio 1.0-1.5:1 → historically ambiguous zone
Ratio <0.8:1 → 15-25 year painful adjustment period
Current estimated ratio: 1.0-1.3:1
Time-varying: ratio may decline as agent capabilities mature
current_assessment: AI sits in the historically ambiguous zone at 1.0-1.3:1. More a monitoring framework than a confident prediction. The time-varying nature (ratio declining as capabilities improve) is an important non-obvious consideration.
limitations: Calibrated from only n=5-8 GPTs
Depends heavily on survey data of uncertain quality
Ratio is time-varying and may shift rapidly with capability improvements
Does not distinguish within-sector from across-sector effects

purpose: Classifies EM economies by their AI-era vulnerability/opportunity profile for FX analysis
components: Segment A: Supply chain beneficiaries (Taiwan, Korea) — semiconductor/hardware exposure, strong current accounts
Segment B: Services-threatened (Philippines, India BPO, Bangladesh) — vulnerable to AI automation of exported cognitive services
Segment C: Commodity-adjacent — indirect exposure through AI energy demand and investment cycle effects
Distinct FX factor exposures for each segment
current_assessment: Directional divergence already observable (TWD/KRW outperforming PHP/BDT since 2023). FDI flows support segmentation. Analytically clean but boundaries are fuzzy (India spans A and B) and operationalization requires unavailable data.
limitations: Boundaries between segments are fuzzy
Operationalization requires data not currently available
No independent agent corroboration
India spanning segments A and B limits clean categorization
A research agenda, not yet an investable framework

purpose: Explains dollar overvaluation through technology investment inflows independent of rate differentials
components: Technology investment inflows tracked via TIC data ($40-60B/month)
EPFR fund flow data ($300B+ into US tech funds)
Dot-com parallel (1997-2001): DXY +25% when Fed not systematically tighter
Captive flow premium (3-5% of overvaluation) from US-China tech bifurcation
Monitorable signposts: capex-to-revenue ratio, foreign equity ownership, DXY-PPP deviation
current_assessment: The capex dollar is currently the dominant FX regime factor, operating orthogonally to rate differentials. This explains why DXY remains elevated despite 100-150bp of Fed cuts.
limitations: n=1 historical analogue (dot-com) with different fiscal/geopolitical backdrop
Captive flow premium magnitude uncertain
Dollar-tech correlation may reflect common factors, not causal chain
Technology landscape evolves rapidly — European/Asian alternatives could gain share

purpose: Models the terminal rate distribution under discrete AI productivity outcomes
components: AI bust scenario: terminal rate 2.0-2.5%
AI muddle-through (current OIS pricing): 3.4-3.7%
AI productivity realized: terminal rate 4.5-5.0%
200-300bp gap between extreme scenarios
Potential 2-3x mispricing in OTM swaption wings (15-20x claim refuted)
current_assessment: Bimodality is structurally real given discrete AI outcomes. OIS curve prices the muddle-through central mode. Valuable as a conceptual tool but specific trade expressions require careful sizing given that uncertain probabilities propagate enormously.
limitations: Treats AI outcomes as discrete when they may be continuous
Swaptions price all macro scenarios, not just AI-conditional
Uncertain scenario probabilities (30-40% range = 10pp uncertainty)
Carry costs of holding convergence positions erode theoretical edge
15-20x mispricing claim refuted — 2-3x is upper bound

purpose: Uses the second derivative of credit (change in new credit flow) to predict growth inflections
components: Biggs-Mayer-Pick (2009) framework
Bank lending credit impulse
Non-bank credit offset (private credit, fintech — 25-30% of consumer origination)
Total credit impulse including shadow banking (BIS data)
Track record: correctly predicted 2008, European crisis, 2020 recovery, 2023-2024 resilience
current_assessment: Bank lending credit impulse decelerating since mid-2024. Non-bank credit partially offsets. Total credit impulse still positive but declining. Signals growth deceleration masked by fiscal offsets.
limitations: Measuring total credit impulse including shadow banking is increasingly difficult
Fintech and private credit channels may not respond to same drivers as bank lending
Real-time utility reduced by measurement difficulty
Kalecki fiscal floor may override credit impulse signal

purpose: Models the feedback loop between stock prices and reported profitability through stock-based compensation
components: Rising stock prices → lower perceived SBC cost per option/RSU
Lower SBC cost → better reported non-GAAP margins
Better margins → higher stock prices
'SBC vega': sensitivity of reported margins to stock price
Creates GAAP vs. non-GAAP P/E gap (10-15 turns for pure-play AI, 1.5-2.0 turns for S&P 500)
current_assessment: The reflexive loop is currently amplifying apparent profitability. Aggregate Mag-7 SBC ~$75-80B annually. The mechanism works in reverse during drawdowns.
limitations: Quantifying the exact magnitude of the distortion requires knowing the true cost of SBC
The reflexive loop may persist indefinitely if markets remain in expansion
GAAP accounting partially captures SBC cost but market focuses on non-GAAP
Does not predict when the loop reverses

purpose: Quantifies how AI concentration distorts financial conditions indices used for monetary policy calibration
components: Cap-weighted FCI equity component captures top-decile firm reality
Median firm conditions 75-100bp tighter than FCI suggests
Cap-weighted vs. equal-weighted S&P 500 divergence of 1000-1500bp cumulative
Top 10 firms: 35% index weight, 3-4% employment
NFIB and regional Fed surveys show more stress than aggregate data
current_assessment: FCI is systematically biased toward loose readings due to AI concentration. Creates cliff risk where policy appears appropriate until median-firm stress cascades into aggregate data.
limitations: 75-100bp FCI distortion is model-dependent
Alternative FCI constructions (equal-weighted, employment-weighted) are not standardized
Unclear whether the Fed is aware of and adjusting for this distortion
The cliff risk prediction assumes median-firm stress eventually cascades, which is unproven

purpose: Analyzes how AI value concentration in 5-7 companies creates systemic macro dependencies
components: Winner-take-all as partial reconciliation of cross-asset signals
Kalecki mechanism linking narrow capex to aggregate profits
Financial conditions reflexivity loop (35% AI index weight embeds AI capex momentum)
Systemic dependence on small number of corporate investment decisions
Slow burn as modal outcome vs. sudden stop
current_assessment: The winner-take-all structure is currently load-bearing for the entire macro regime via Kalecki profit identity and FCI construction. This transforms sector risk into macro regime risk.
limitations: No mechanism identified for why the structure would remain stable indefinitely
The 2022 tech drawdown counterexample suggests cascades are not automatic
Slow burn modal outcome depends on unprecedented corporate self-restraint
The structure's stability has not been tested under a genuine AI-thesis-breaking event

purpose: Estimates systematic Fed policy rate bias from AI-related productivity mismeasurement
components: BLS service sector quality adjustment gaps
Intangible capital undercounting in GDP/productivity statistics
1990s revision precedent (1.0-1.5pp upward productivity revision after the fact)
50-100bp estimated Taylor rule bias
AI Solow paradox window: 2-4 year period of maximum policy error risk
current_assessment: If AI is genuinely boosting productivity but measurement fails to capture it, the Taylor rule output gap estimate is too pessimistic and the implied policy rate is too low — risking inflation. Conversely, if AI is not boosting productivity, current pricing is correct. The Solow paradox window creates maximum uncertainty.
limitations: Mismeasurement magnitude is itself unmeasurable in real time
1990s revision precedent may not apply — different statistical methodology now
Four indistinguishable data interpretations require opposite policy responses
The 50-100bp bias estimate is highly model-dependent

purpose: Breaks down AI productivity transmission into five discrete steps with distinct speeds
components: Step 1: Technology availability (AI models exist and are accessible)
Step 2: Firm adoption (companies deploy AI tools)
Step 3: Task-level gains (individual tasks performed faster/better)
Step 4: Firm-level gains (overall firm productivity improves)
Step 5: Sector/aggregate TFP (macro-visible productivity acceleration)
current_assessment: Step 1 is complete, Step 2 is well underway but uneven across sectors, Step 3 is observable in early-adopter tasks, Steps 4-5 are not yet measurable. Anchoring asymmetry: bulls anchor to Step 2 speed, bears anchor to Steps 4-5 lags.
limitations: Assumes sequential progression which may not hold strictly
Each step has different measurement challenges
The anchoring asymmetry critique applies to the framework itself — which step is most informative?
Does not predict speeds, only identifies where bottlenecks exist

purpose: Classifies general-purpose technologies by whether they can be adopted with or without organizational restructuring
components: Plug-and-play GPTs: can be adopted within existing organizational structures (incremental efficiency gains)
Reorganization-dependent GPTs: require fundamental restructuring of work processes, management, and organizational design
Reorganization is 60-70% of total productivity lag historically
AI is reorganization-dependent but may partially self-accelerate Phase 2 (recursive reorganization)
current_assessment: AI is a reorganization-dependent GPT, which explains the long productivity lag. The key unresolvable question is whether AI's ability to assist in its own organizational restructuring compresses the historical timeline.
limitations: Binary classification may be too coarse — GPTs exist on a spectrum
Recursive reorganization hypothesis is untestable until it either happens or doesn't
Does not specify which organizations need what type of reorganization
Historical analogy base is small (4 GPTs)
